summary: The Gemara discusses the weight of the crimson thread used with the red heifer and the Yom Kippur goat, resolves a dispute about whether a non-priest may slaughter the red heifer, digresses on the customs of the people of Jericho, and concludes with the ish iti's impurity from carrying the Azazel goat.

display.whats: How much did the crimson thread weigh, and when does the ish iti become impure?
display.hint: Two disputes run in parallel: the weight of the crimson thread (for parah vs. Yom Kippur goat) and whether a non-priest may slaughter the parah. R. Yochanan boldly contradicts his own teacher on the latter.
display.title: Crimson Thread Weights and the Non-Priest Slaughter Dispute

learning.ahaMoment: R. Yochanan publicly contradicts his own teacher because he is convinced that non-priest slaughter is always valid - one of the few cases where a student overrules his teacher on record.
learning.memoryAnchor: Crimson thread: 10 zuz for the red cow, 2 sela for the Yom Kippur goat - and R. Yochanan tells a tanna to 'go teach that outside' when the tanna claims otherwise.
learning.learnerQuestion: How much did the crimson thread weigh for each rite, and may a non-priest validly slaughter the red heifer?
learning.coreTension: A baraita's crimson-thread weights for the red heifer and the Yom Kippur goat sit alongside a live dispute over whether the parah may be slaughtered by a non-priest, with R. Yochanan breaking from his own teacher's ruling that such slaughter is invalid.
learning.coreMove: R. Yochanan rejects the baraita's claim and the position of his own teacher R. Shimon ben Yehotzadak, ruling that non-priest slaughter of the parah has never been found invalid, while the digression on Jericho's customs and the ish iti's impurity round out the daf's practical details.

takeaway.text: The crimson thread weighed ten zuz for the red heifer and two sela for the Yom Kippur goat; R. Yochanan rules that non-priest slaughter of the parah is valid, even overruling his own teacher on the matter.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may conflate the non-priest slaughter dispute (about the parah) with the Yom Kippur goat rite, since both share the crimson-thread theme on this daf, when the slaughter debate is specifically about parah.

argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna
argumentFlow.text: Weight of crimson thread: 10 zuz for parah, 2 sela for Yom Kippur goat.
argumentFlow.label: Citation: Weight of crimson thread: 10 zuz for

argumentFlow.speaker: Amoraim
argumentFlow.text: R. Shimon ben Chalafta and Rabbis dispute a related weight question.
argumentFlow.label: Dispute: R. Shimon ben Chalafta and Rabbis dispute

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Verse 'Elazar the priest shall slaughter it' - implies priest required for parah.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Verse 'Elazar the priest shall slaughter it'

argumentFlow.speaker: Amoraim
argumentFlow.text: R. Ami: non-priest parah slaughter valid. Rav: invalid.
argumentFlow.label: Dispute: R. Ami: non-priest parah slaughter valid. Rav:

argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: We have not found slaughter by non-priest to be invalid - rejects the baraita.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: We have not found slaughter by non-priest

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: People of Jericho: three permitted customs, three forbidden ones.
argumentFlow.label: Narrative: People of Jericho: three permitted customs, three

argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The ish iti who sends the goat becomes impure by carrying it.
argumentFlow.label: Ruling: The ish iti who sends the goat

argumentFlow.speaker: Tanna/R. Akiva
argumentFlow.text: R. Akiva: impurity from moment of sending. Sages: from when goat reaches wilderness.
argumentFlow.label: Dispute: R. Akiva: impurity from moment of sending.

misconceptions.misconception: The dispute over whether a non-priest may slaughter applies to the Yom Kippur goat sacrifice, since both are discussed together with the crimson thread.
misconceptions.correction: The non-priest slaughter dispute is specifically about the parah adumah (red heifer); the Yom Kippur goat is a separate rite, linked on this daf only by the shared crimson-thread theme.

visualizableElements.label: parah thread weight 10 zuz vs Yom Kippur thread 2 sela

quizSeeds.question: What was the weight of the crimson thread for the Yom Kippur goat?

quizSeeds.answer: Because the tanna claimed non-priest parah slaughter is invalid, and R. Yochanan held we have never found non-priest slaughter to be invalid.
quizSeeds.question: Why did R. Yochanan tell the tanna to 'go teach outside'?

sugyot.finalRuling: The ish iti becomes impure by carrying the goat; he must immerse and wait until nightfall.

glossary.en: The designated man who escorts and sends the Azazel goat to the wilderness cliff on Yom Kippur.
Red heifer - a cow whose ashes purify those contaminated by a corpse.
One who has immersed in a mikveh but not yet waited until nightfall for full purification.

rashiTranslations.en: The weight of the crimson thread used for the red heifer is ten zuz; for the Yom Kippur goat, two sela.
R. Shimon ben Chalafta and the Rabbis disagree about a related matter.
R. Yirmeyah of Difti: this is not about the parah strip but about the goat strip.
R. Yitzchak: I heard a distinction between two strips - one before, one after.
An amoraic dispute: slaughter of the parah by a non-priest - R. Ami says valid, Rav says invalid.
The verse says 'and Elazar the priest shall slaughter it' - priest is required for parah.
But did we not learn that all slaughterings are valid if done by a non-priest?
R. Yochanan to the tanna: go teach that outside - we have not found slaughter invalid with a non-priest.
The Gemara notes R. Yochanan didn't even follow his own teacher R. Shimon ben Yehotzadak.
R. Shimon ben Yehotzadak said: slaughter of parah by non-priest is invalid; R. Yochanan disagrees.
The people of Jericho did six things - three they were permitted, three they were not.
They permitted: grafting carobs on Shabbat eve of sabbatical year, gathering scattered stalks, and using Jericho pine.
They forbade: eating fallen fruit on Shabbat, forgetting the Omer, permitting crop stalks.
The Sages did not agree but did not object; the things permitted they were right about.
Grafting carobs: R. Yehuda says there is no prohibition; the Sages permitted them retroactively.
Scattered stalks: a poor person may gather them; the people of Jericho allowed anyone.
Pine branches: they used them on Pesach without objection - but the matter is disputed.
The forbidden acts: eating fallen fruit they should not have done on Shabbat.
Forgetting the Omer: Rebbi permitted it in his locale but not universally.
Permitting crop stalks before Shabbat: they relied on lenient opinions but the Sages objected.
Another teaching about Jericho customs and what the Sages permitted or forbade.
Summary: some Jericho practices were corrected by the Sages and some remained.
The ish iti (the designated man) who takes the goat becomes impure through carrying it.
Even though the goat is alive, it makes the handler impure - because it is a carrier of impurity.
The goat is designated for the wilderness; its very purpose transmits impurity status.
This teaches: a living creature can convey impurity upon the person who carries it.
Discussion of what exactly makes the ish iti impure - the goat itself or the act of sending.
The verse 'and he who sends the goat shall wash his garments' indicates deliberate impurity designation.
R. Akiva: the ish iti becomes impure from the moment he takes the goat and sends it.
The Sages: he becomes impure only when the goat has reached the wilderness.
Practical difference: if he bathed mid-journey and the goat died - is he still impure?
The debate about when the impurity takes effect corresponds to when the goat's mission is complete.
Both opinions agree the ish iti must immerse and wait until nightfall.
Connection to the general principle: items sent for impure purposes convey impurity.
Final ruling: the ish iti is impure until evening and must immerse.
Summary of the ish iti impurity laws and their biblical basis.
The Gemara concludes the discussion of Yom Kippur goat and attendant impurity.
Transition to the next Mishna topic regarding the High Priest's subsequent actions.
The bull's blood and the goat's blood will be handled next in the avodah sequence.
Review: the parah slaughter dispute and ish iti impurity have been resolved.
Additional details about the 10-booth journey stations to the Tsuk cliff.
The booths are spaced along the route; provisions are made at each.
People would accompany the ish iti from station to station as honor.
At the Tsuk: the ish iti pushes the goat off backward; it tumbles and is killed.
The crimson thread tied to the goat turns white - a sign of atonement (Is. 1:18).
If the thread does not turn white, this was seen as a bad omen in later generations.
The Gemara notes: in Shimon HaTzaddik's era the thread always turned white.
After his death, results became unreliable; eventually they stopped relying on the sign.
The Gemara will now proceed to the next chapter of Mishna Yoma.
End of discussion of the goat ceremony; transition to incense offerings.
Summary of 42a: thread weight, parah slaughter dispute, ish iti impurity.
The page concludes the goat ceremony material and anticipates incense discussion.
